english: Grilled Bread with Tomato and Olive Oil

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving of bruschetta is approximately 3–4 pieces (about 150 g), consisting of grilled rustic bread slices topped with fresh tomato, basil, garlic, and extra virgin olive oil.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Bruschetta and component ingredients (bread, tomato, olive oil, basil, garlic)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for bruschetta as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are adapted from the input record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central component entries for Italian bread (FDC ID 325871), raw tomatoes (FDC ID 170457), extra virgin olive oil (FDC ID 171413), fresh basil (FDC ID 172232), and raw garlic (FDC ID 169230). Values are plausible for a grilled bread and fresh tomato topping preparation. Confidence is medium due to composite estimation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for bruschetta as a composite dish. GI of 57 is estimated using the analogous food 'white bread with olive oil and tomato topping' from the University of Sydney GI Database, which reports GI values for white bread in the range 70–75 (GI Database entry) reduced by the presence of olive oil and acidic tomato toppings, consistent with published evidence that fat and acid additions lower the glycemic response of bread (Brand-Miller et al., Diabetes Care 2008). Rustic/sourdough-style bread further reduces GI toward the medium range. GL per serving calculated as: GI (57) × carbohydrate per serving (35 g from 150 g portion) / 100 = 20.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 266.7 mg/100 g; a 150 g serving delivers approximately 400 mg sodium, which is 17–20% of the 2,000 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation and limit portion size to one serving; the potassium contribution from tomatoes (~200 mg/100 g) and the vasodilatory polyphenols in extra virgin olive oil partially offset the sodium load, but cumulative sodium from the bread base warrants caution.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate content 23.3 g/100 g with a medium estimated GI of 57; a 150 g serving delivers approximately 35 g carbohydrate and a glycemic load of 20, which is in the high GL range per serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit bruschetta to one serving (150 g) and choose sourdough or wholegrain rustic bread to reduce the glycemic response; the dietary fibre (2.7 g/100 g) and extra virgin olive oil slow glucose absorption, but total carbohydrate per serving must be counted within the daily carbohydrate budget.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is very low at 1.33 g/100 g; the dominant fat is monounsaturated fat from extra virgin olive oil (~6.5 g/100 g estimated), which is cardioprotective; cholesterol is 0 mg/100 g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Bruschetta prepared with extra virgin olive oil is consistent with a Mediterranean dietary pattern and is suitable for individuals managing cardiovascular disease when consumed in moderate portions; sodium intake should be monitored as a 150 g serving contributes approximately 400 mg sodium.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Extra virgin olive oil provides oleocanthal (anti-inflammatory polyphenol) and MUFAs; tomatoes and basil contribute vitamin C (13.3 mg/100 g) and antioxidants; saturated fat is very low at 1.33 g/100 g.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Bruschetta is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its anti-inflammatory ingredient profile; patients who experience symptom flares after consuming nightshade vegetables (tomatoes) should monitor their individual response.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bruschetta (Vegetarian) is a classic Italian appetizer consisting of slices of rustic or sourdough-style bread that are grilled until golden and crisp, then topped with a mixture of fresh diced tomatoes, fresh basil leaves, dried oregano, minced garlic, and organic extra virgin olive oil. The dish originates from central Italy and is a staple of Italian antipasto culture. The texture contrasts a crunchy, charred bread base with the juicy, soft tomato topping. The flavour profile is bright, herbaceous, and savoury, with the fruity bitterness of extra virgin olive oil complementing the acidity of fresh tomatoes and the aromatic punch of garlic and basil. Nutritionally, bruschetta provides moderate energy (200 kcal/100 g), moderate carbohydrates (23.3 g/100 g) primarily from the bread base, and a favourable fat profile dominated by monounsaturated fats from olive oil (saturated fat only 1.33 g/100 g). Dietary fibre is 2.67 g/100 g and vitamin C is 13.3 mg/100 g from the fresh tomatoes. Sodium is 266.7 mg/100 g, which is borderline for hypertensive individuals. The dish is naturally vegan, vegetarian, dairy-free, and contains no cholesterol. It is suitable for Mediterranean-style dietary patterns and is clinically relevant for cardiovascular health due to its olive oil content. A standard serving is 3–4 pieces (approximately 150 g), delivering approximately 300 kcal and 35 g carbohydrate. The estimated glycemic index is 57 (medium), moderated by olive oil and tomato acidity. Bruschetta is commonly served as a starter or light snack in Italian and Italian-inspired restaurant menus across Singapore and Southeast Asia.

Italian appetizer with tomato and olive oil

notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates adapted from USDA FoodData Central component ingredients (Italian bread, raw tomatoes, extra virgin olive oil, basil, garlic) cross-referenced against the input ai_generated record. No single USDA FoodData Central entry exists for bruschetta as a prepared dish. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g (6.5) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.2) are estimated from the olive oil content of the dish using USDA FoodData Central data for extra virgin olive oil (FDC ID 171413); these are approximations. 3. potassium_mg (200.0) is estimated from tomato and garlic content using USDA FoodData Central component data; this is an approximation. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this composite bruschetta preparation are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024. 5. GI of 57 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for bruschetta exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. The input ethnic_tags included 'Pasta', 'Pizza', and 'Chicken Dish' which are not applicable to bruschetta and have been removed. country_of_origin was listed as 'Singapore' in the input; this has been corrected to 'Italy' as bruschetta is an Italian dish served in Singapore. 7. FoodEx2 code A0CJW (toasted bread) is used as the closest defensible anchor; no dedicated FoodEx2 composite dish code for bruschetta was identified. 8. trans_fat_g is 0.0: confirmed as negligible/zero for this preparation using plant-based oils only; no industrial trans fats present. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero as this is a fully plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients.
